title: The Man Who Stole the Mona Lisa and Made Her Famous
description:
The Theft of the Mona Lisa (1911) — On August 21, 1911, a former Louvre employee named Vincenzo Peruggia walked into the museum, removed the world's most famous painting from the wall, hid it under his coat, and walked out. The theft turned the Mona Lisa from a respected artwork into the most famous painting on Earth and sparked a manhunt that captivated the world.

Sources: Paris Prefecture of Police investigation records, Louvre Museum archives, Italian court records from Florence, Le Petit Parisien and other French newspaper archives.

DISCLAIMER: This video is for educational and documentary purposes only. All information presented is sourced from publicly available records, news reports, and official statements. Viewer discretion is advised.

#truecrime #monalisa #artheist #louvre #paris #vincenzoperuggia #leonardodavinci #theft #museum #darkfiles
case_name: The Theft of the Mona Lisa
sources: Paris Prefecture of Police investigation records

segments.voice: On the morning of August 22, 1911, a painter named Louis Beroud arrived at the Louvre Museum in Paris to sketch a copy of the Mona Lisa, as he had done many times before. When he reached the Salon Carre, where Leonardo da Vinci's masterpiece had hung for over a century, he found only four iron pegs on a bare section of wall. The painting was gone. Beroud alerted a guard, who assumed the painting had been taken to the museum's photography studio. Hours passed before anyone realized the truth. The Mona Lisa had been stolen.
segments.visual: empty museum wall with hooks classical gallery

segments.voice: The theft of the Mona Lisa from the Louvre was an event of such audacity that many people initially refused to believe it had happened. According to the Paris Prefecture of Police records, the painting's disappearance was not reported to authorities until the following afternoon, a full twenty-six hours after it was likely taken. The Louvre's security in 1911 was remarkably lax by modern standards. The museum employed only a handful of guards to watch over thousands of artworks, and the building had multiple entrances and corridors that were poorly monitored.
segments.visual: grand museum corridor classical architecture empty

segments.voice: According to Louvre administrative records, the museum was closed to the public every Monday for maintenance and cleaning. The theft is believed to have occurred on Monday, August 21st, when the building was populated only by staff and workmen. Whoever took the painting exploited this reduced security window. The Mona Lisa, painted on a poplar wood panel measuring approximately thirty inches by twenty-one inches, weighed about eighteen pounds in its protective glass case. It was small enough for a single person to carry, a fact that made the crime deceptively simple.
segments.visual: ornate museum room with classical paintings

segments.voice: When news of the theft became public, it ignited a media frenzy that spread across the globe. According to newspaper archives from the period, the theft was front-page news in every major city. In Paris, crowds gathered at the Louvre to stare at the empty space where the Mona Lisa had hung, a phenomenon that museum officials had never seen before. People came not to see art but to see the absence of art. The empty wall became a spectacle in itself, and more people visited the Louvre in the week after the theft than had come in the week before it.
segments.visual: crowd of people at museum entrance historic

segments.voice: The Paris Prefecture of Police launched what was described as the largest art crime investigation in French history. According to police records, the investigation was led by Prefect Louis Lepine, who deployed over sixty detectives to the case. Every known art thief, fence, and dealer in Paris was questioned. The borders of France were closed for a week as authorities searched outgoing trains, ships, and vehicles. Interpol, then in its infancy, issued alerts to police forces across Europe. The French government offered a reward for the painting's return. Despite all this effort, there were no credible leads.
segments.visual: vintage detective office 1900s era desk

segments.voice: Among the most famous suspects in the early investigation was the poet Guillaume Apollinaire, a prominent figure in the Parisian avant-garde. According to police records, Apollinaire was arrested on September 7, 1911, after his secretary was found in possession of small sculptures stolen from the Louvre. Under interrogation, Apollinaire panicked and implicated his friend, the young Spanish painter Pablo Picasso. Both men were questioned by police. Picasso reportedly wept during his interrogation, denying any knowledge of the Mona Lisa theft. Both were eventually cleared and released.
segments.visual: art studio with paintings and supplies

segments.voice: According to the investigation files, the Picasso and Apollinaire connection stemmed from a separate series of thefts from the Louvre committed by Apollinaire's secretary, a Belgian adventurer named Gery Pieret. Pieret had stolen small Iberian sculptures from the museum and sold them to Picasso, who used them as inspiration for his groundbreaking painting 'Les Demoiselles d'Avignon.' When news of the Mona Lisa theft broke, Pieret went to a newspaper and confessed to stealing the sculptures, hoping for attention. This led police to Apollinaire and then to Picasso, but neither was connected to the main theft.
segments.visual: sculpture display in museum classical art

segments.voice: As months passed with no breakthrough, the investigation stalled and public interest waned. According to press reports from the period, the Mona Lisa's disappearance became a source of national embarrassment for France. The director of the Louvre was forced to resign. Cartoonists and satirists mocked the police and museum security. Conspiracy theories proliferated, with some suggesting the painting had been stolen by a wealthy American collector, others claiming it was a political plot, and still others arguing that the painting had never been real to begin with and the Louvre had been displaying a forgery.
segments.visual: vintage newspaper political cartoon illustration

segments.voice: What no one knew during the two years the painting was missing was that the thief was hiding in plain sight. According to the confession he later gave to police, Vincenzo Peruggia was an Italian handyman who had been employed by the Louvre to install protective glass cases over several paintings, including the Mona Lisa. On the morning of Monday, August 21, 1911, Peruggia entered the museum wearing a white workman's smock, identical to those worn by museum maintenance staff. He went directly to the Salon Carre, where the Mona Lisa hung.
segments.visual: man in work clothes vintage early 1900s

segments.voice: According to Peruggia's account and the subsequent investigation, he waited until the gallery was empty, then lifted the Mona Lisa from its iron pegs and carried it to a nearby service stairwell. In the stairwell, he removed the painting from its protective glass case and wooden frame, wrapped the bare panel in his smock, and tucked it under his arm. He then walked down the stairs, through a corridor, and out a side door of the museum. When the door handle stuck, he removed the doorknob and continued. He walked out of the Louvre with the Mona Lisa under his coat in broad daylight.
segments.visual: old stairwell interior building historic

segments.voice: Peruggia took the painting to his small apartment near the Louvre, where he hid it in a trunk with a false bottom. According to police records compiled after his arrest, Peruggia lived with the painting hidden in his room for over two years. He was questioned by police during the investigation because his fingerprint was found on the glass case left behind in the stairwell. However, according to the investigation files, when officers visited his apartment, they accepted his alibi that he had been at work on the day of the theft without conducting a thorough search of his belongings.
segments.visual: small vintage apartment room with trunk chest

segments.voice: For twenty-eight months, the Mona Lisa sat in a trunk in a tiny Parisian apartment while the world's police forces searched for it. According to Peruggia's later statements, he occasionally took the painting out to look at it, marveling that he possessed what was arguably the most sought-after object on earth. He apparently believed that what he had done was not a crime but a patriotic act. Peruggia was convinced that the Mona Lisa had been stolen from Italy by Napoleon and that he was returning it to its rightful homeland. In this belief, he was historically incorrect.
segments.visual: painting covered with cloth hidden storage

segments.voice: In November 1913, more than two years after the theft, Peruggia made his move. According to Italian court records, he traveled from Paris to Florence by train, carrying the Mona Lisa in a wooden trunk. He contacted Alfredo Geri, a well-known Florentine art dealer, and offered to sell him the painting for five hundred thousand lire, the equivalent of approximately two million dollars today. Peruggia told Geri he wanted the painting returned to Italy and that the money was simply to cover his expenses. Geri agreed to meet and examine the painting.
segments.visual: old train station platform vintage railway

segments.voice: According to the account given by Alfredo Geri, Peruggia invited him to his room at the Hotel Tripoli-Italia in Florence on December 10, 1913. Peruggia pulled the wooden trunk from under his bed, removed various items including old shoes and clothing, and then lifted a false bottom to reveal the painting wrapped in red silk. Geri immediately recognized the Mona Lisa. He asked Peruggia if he could take the painting to the Uffizi Gallery for authentication by Giovanni Poggi, the gallery's director. Peruggia agreed, apparently not suspecting what would happen next.
segments.visual: vintage hotel room interior simple furnishings

segments.voice: Geri and Poggi examined the painting at the Uffizi Gallery and confirmed its authenticity through the inventory number painted on the back of the panel and by comparing it against known photographs. According to the official verification report, the painting was the genuine Mona Lisa, undamaged and in remarkably good condition despite its two-year adventure in a trunk. Geri and Poggi then contacted the police. When Peruggia returned to the Uffizi expecting to collect his payment, he was arrested by Italian carabinieri. He appeared genuinely shocked that he was being treated as a criminal.
segments.visual: Renaissance art gallery in Florence Italy

segments.voice: Vincenzo Peruggia's trial in Florence in June 1914 was a sensation that drew international attention. According to Italian court transcripts, Peruggia's defense attorney portrayed his client as a patriotic Italian who had simply tried to return a national treasure to its homeland. Peruggia testified that he loved Italy and could not bear to see Italian art treasures displayed in a French museum. He claimed he had acted alone and that his motives were purely nationalistic. The Italian public was largely sympathetic, and many viewed Peruggia as a hero rather than a criminal.
segments.visual: Italian courtroom historic architecture interior

segments.voice: According to the court records, Peruggia was found guilty of the theft but received a remarkably lenient sentence: one year and fifteen days in prison, later reduced to seven months and nine days on appeal. The light sentence reflected both the court's sympathy for his stated patriotic motives and the fact that the painting had been recovered undamaged. Peruggia served his sentence and was released. He returned to France, worked as a house painter, served in the Italian army during World War One, married, had a daughter, and lived quietly until his death in 1925 at the age of forty-four.
segments.visual: old prison cell interior iron bars window

segments.voice: Peruggia's claim that he acted alone has been questioned by researchers for over a century. According to some historical investigations, a con artist known as the Marquis de Valfierno may have been the mastermind behind the theft. According to this theory, Valfierno commissioned the theft so that he could sell forged copies of the Mona Lisa to wealthy collectors around the world. With the original missing, the forgeries would be impossible to disprove. Valfierno allegedly sold six fake Mona Lisas to collectors in different countries before the real painting was recovered. This theory remains unproven.
segments.visual: art forgery painting copying in studio

segments.voice: Before the Mona Lisa was returned to France, the Italian government displayed it in a triumphant tour of Italian cities. According to museum records, the painting was exhibited in Florence, Rome, and Milan, where enormous crowds lined up for hours to see it. The Italian press celebrated the painting's brief return to Italian soil. The painting was finally returned to France in January 1914 and rehung in the Louvre on January 4th, where it was greeted by massive crowds and intensive new security measures. The Mona Lisa had come home, but she was no longer the same painting she had been before.
segments.visual: crowd of people waiting in line at museum

segments.voice: The theft fundamentally transformed the Mona Lisa's cultural status. According to art historians, before 1911, the Mona Lisa was respected among art connoisseurs as a fine example of Renaissance portraiture, but it was not considered the world's most famous painting. That distinction belonged to works like Raphael's Sistine Madonna or the Venus de Milo. The theft, and the massive media coverage it generated, elevated the Mona Lisa to a level of fame that no artwork had ever achieved. The theft made the painting a global celebrity, a status it retains to this day.
segments.visual: museum exhibit hall with visitors viewing art

segments.voice: According to cultural historians, the Mona Lisa's theft also created the modern phenomenon of art as public spectacle. Before 1911, museums were quiet places visited by educated elites and serious students. The crowds that gathered at the Louvre to see the empty wall, and later to see the returned painting, represented something new: mass public engagement with visual art. The theft demonstrated that a painting could become news, that art could be dramatic, and that the story surrounding a work could become as important as the work itself. Peruggia inadvertently invented the blockbuster exhibition.
segments.visual: packed museum exhibition modern visitors

segments.voice: The Louvre implemented dramatic security improvements after the painting's return. According to museum records, the Mona Lisa was placed behind bulletproof glass and given its own dedicated security system with alarms and cameras. Over the decades, security has been upgraded multiple times. In 1956, someone threw acid at the painting, damaging the lower section. Later that year, a visitor hurled a rock at the glass. In 2009, a woman threw a ceramic cup. In 2022, a man smeared cake on the glass. Each attack prompted further security enhancements. The painting is now one of the most protected objects on earth.
segments.visual: museum security guard standing at post

segments.voice: Vincenzo Peruggia's fingerprint, the one found on the glass case in the Louvre stairwell, represents one of the great missed opportunities in criminal investigation history. According to French police records, Peruggia had been fingerprinted when he was arrested for a minor offense in Paris in 1909. His prints were on file at the Prefecture of Police. However, the fingerprint comparison system in 1911 was organized alphabetically by name, not by print pattern. Since investigators did not know the thief's name, they could not find his prints in their files. The answer was there all along, unreachable.
segments.visual: fingerprint close up on glass surface

segments.voice: The Mona Lisa's fame has grown exponentially in the century since the theft. According to the Louvre's official statistics, the painting now receives approximately ten million visitors per year, making it the single most viewed artwork on the planet. Visitors routinely wait in lines that stretch for hours to spend a few moments in front of the painting's bulletproof case. Many visitors photograph the painting rather than looking at it directly, creating the paradox of a masterpiece that is seen by millions but truly observed by remarkably few. The painting has transcended art to become pure icon.
segments.visual: tourists taking photos at museum crowded

segments.voice: Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa between approximately 1503 and 1519 in Italy. The subject is believed to be Lisa Gherardini, the wife of a Florentine merchant. According to art historians, Leonardo brought the unfinished painting with him when he moved to France in 1516 at the invitation of King Francis the First. After Leonardo's death in 1519, the painting entered the French royal collection. It was never stolen by Napoleon, as Peruggia believed. It came to France in the hands of its creator over three centuries before Napoleon was even born. Peruggia's patriotic justification was built on a misunderstanding.
segments.visual: Renaissance painting artist workshop studio

segments.voice: According to contemporary art insurance assessments, the Mona Lisa is effectively priceless. The painting was assessed at one hundred million dollars in 1962 for insurance purposes during its loan to the United States, an amount that would equal nearly one billion dollars adjusted for inflation today. However, the French government has declared the painting a national treasure that can never be sold. No insurance company would underwrite it for its true value. The painting has transcended the concept of monetary worth. It exists in a category of its own, an object beyond price, beyond replacement, beyond duplication.
segments.visual: gold vault door luxury safe room

segments.voice: The story of the Mona Lisa's theft is ultimately a story about how crime creates fame. According to cultural theorists, Vincenzo Peruggia committed one of the simplest major crimes in history: a man picked up a painting and walked out a door. Yet that simple act transformed the Mona Lisa from a respected artwork into the most recognizable image in human civilization. It made Peruggia himself a figure of enduring fascination. And it proved that sometimes the most powerful force in creating a legend is not genius or beauty, but the simple, dramatic act of taking something away and making the world wonder where it went.
segments.visual: empty picture frame spotlight on wall dramatic
